title: esus’ first recorded prayer part 3
scripture_text: “At that time Jesus said, “I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this is what you were pleased to do. All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. “Come to me all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. for my yoke is easy and my burden is light.”
devotional:
We must remember the context of these words; Jesus had to condemn the cities of Korazin, Bethsaida and Capernaum. This was not easy for him to do, so in response to this demanding experience, Jesus turned to his father in prayer. The attitude he expressed in prayer to his father, was the yoke he was referring to. The yoke that Jesus wore was the yoke of humble submission to his father. Our yoke is whatever we bow our heads to.

We have considered the prayer of Jesus and we've noticed that: -

Jesus found comfort in his relationship with his father.

Jesus was completely submissive to his Fathers will.

Jesus believed in the sovereignty of God.

Jesus acknowledged the ways of the kingdom.

Jesus knew the importance of being a Son.

These attitudes make up the yoke that Jesus wore. Imagine what would happen to a man who did not have these attitudes to govern his life. He would have the weight of the world on his shoulders. He would feel responsible for being successful, he could never rest or relax, and his confidence would be in himself, not in God. This is the heavy load Jesus is referring to.

Jesus says two things about how he carries his yoke.

He was gentle. This is the only time in scripture where Jesus speaks about his heart. The word for gentle is the same word that Jesus used in the beatitudes when he said 'Blessed are the meek.'

As we have seen before this is a picture word.

A. A gentle spirit describes a soothing medicine.

B. A gentle spirit refers to a gentle breeze.

C. A gentle spirit refers to a colt that is broken and useful.

D. A gentle spirit refers to a person who treats others with respect, dignity, politeness and courtesy.

E. A gentile spirit was used in much extra-biblical literature to refer to a horse that had been trained. Therefore the idea is "power under control." A horse that is trained is useful and safe. However one that cannot be controlled is useless and dangerous. A medicine that can be controlled can bring healing. However one that cannot be controlled can kill. A wind that blows with controlled speeds is useful to pump water and provide energy. One without control is destructive. A person who can control his words is encouraging and pleasant. A person who is not meek and gentle is out of control.

When our hearts are harsh and hard, we will find it impossible to submit to the yoke of Jesus.
prayer: Father May I demonstrate the gentleness of Jesus.
html:
esus’ first recorded prayer part 3

Matthew 11:25-30 J
“At that time Jesus said, “I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this is what you were pleased to do. All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. “Come to me all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. for my yoke is easy and my burden is light.”

We must remember the context of these words; Jesus had to condemn the cities of Korazin, Bethsaida and Capernaum. This was not easy for him to do, so in response to this demanding experience, Jesus turned to his father in prayer. The attitude he expressed in prayer to his father, was the yoke he was referring to. The yoke that Jesus wore was the yoke of humble submission to his father. Our yoke is whatever we bow our heads to.

We have considered the prayer of Jesus and we've noticed that: -

These attitudes make up the yoke that Jesus wore. Imagine what would happen to a man who did not have these attitudes to govern his life. He would have the weight of the world on his shoulders. He would feel responsible for being successful, he could never rest or relax, and his confidence would be in himself, not in God. This is the heavy load Jesus is referring to.

Jesus says two things about how he carries his yoke.

He was gentle. This is the only time in scripture where Jesus speaks about his heart. The word for gentle is the same word that Jesus used in the beatitudes when he said 'Blessed are the meek.'

As we have seen before this is a picture word.

A. A gentle spirit describes a soothing medicine.

E. A gentile spirit was used in much extra-biblical literature to refer to a horse that had been trained. Therefore the idea is "power under control." A horse that is trained is useful and safe. However one that cannot be controlled is useless and dangerous. A medicine that can be controlled can bring healing. However one that cannot be controlled can kill. A wind that blows with controlled speeds is useful to pump water and provide energy. One without control is destructive. A person who can control his words is encouraging and pleasant. A person who is not meek and gentle is out of control.

When our hearts are harsh and hard, we will find it impossible to submit to the yoke of Jesus.

Prayer: Father May I demonstrate the gentleness of Jesus.
Bible in a year reading plan: -1Samuel 9:11-12:18
